snap_subtitle: Big, comfy wireless with smart binds—great for palm, heavy for twitch FPS.
keytakeaway: Corsair's Ironclaw RGB Wireless is a large, right-handed palm-grip mouse with 10 programmable buttons and both Slipstream 2.4GHz + Bluetooth, making it a strong pick for MMO/MOBA/ARPG hotkeys and easy multi-device swapping. The trade-off is weight: it feels planted and stable, but it’s not the mouse you buy for low-DPI flicks or aim-training when you want something ultra-light.
pcard: **Corsair Ironclaw RGB Wireless** is a big, palm-first ergonomic mouse that shines when you want extra binds without going full MMO-grid. You get Slipstream 2.4GHz, Bluetooth, and USB play-while-charging, plus 10 programmable buttons for abilities, pings, or push-to-talk. If you want a similar “button-heavy” wireless vibe, compare <pop=mouse-97>Logitech G502 X Lightspeed Wireless</pop>; if you want a true MMO side-button wall, step up to <pop=mouse-27>Corsair Scimitar Elite Wireless</pop>.
verdict:
#### Quick take

The Ironclaw RGB Wireless is built around a simple idea: fill the hand, stay comfortable, and give you enough buttons to actually *do things* mid-fight. The shape is wide and palm-friendly (especially for larger hands), with a thumb-side cluster that’s easy to bind for abilities, pings, push-to-talk, or utility shortcuts. In MOBA/MMO/ARPG play, that convenience matters more than shaving grams.

> A comfort-first, right-hand wireless “command mouse” that’s awesome for ability-heavy games—just know it’s heavy, and it’s not chasing modern esports-light trends.

On the connection side, it’s flexible: Slipstream 2.4GHz for “gaming mode,” Bluetooth for quick laptop pairing, and a wired option so you can keep playing while it charges. It’s also the kind of mouse that can double as a work companion—those extra buttons are just as useful for copy/paste, timeline nudges, or app macros as they are for cooldowns.

| Aspect | What you’ll notice |
|---|---|
| Shape | Big, supportive palm feel that suits larger hands best |
| Buttons | 10 total, with easy “bindable” access for skills/macros |
| Weight | Stable and planted—less “flicky” for fast, low-DPI aim |

#### Where it shines
* MMO / MOBA / ARPG setups where extra binds genuinely reduce keyboard juggling  
* Players who prefer palm grip comfort over ultra-light speed  
* Multi-device users who want both 2.4GHz and Bluetooth in one mouse  

#### Trade-offs to know
* The weight is the headline compromise—great for control, but it can feel sluggish in twitchy FPS if you live on big flicks  
* Bluetooth is best treated as “basic mode”; for the deepest remaps/macros, you’ll usually want the 2.4GHz dongle or wired  
* Battery expectations depend heavily on wireless mode and lighting—if you leave RGB on and stay on 2.4GHz, you’ll charge more often  
* Long-term feedback is mixed: comfort gets praise, but scroll-wheel quirks show up often enough to be worth keeping in mind

#### Alternatives worth a look
* Want a lighter, aim-first palm wireless? Check <pop=mouse-128>Razer Deathadder V3 Pro</pop>.  
* Want a similar ergonomic multi-button vibe in another layout? Try <pop=mouse-139>Razer Basilisk V3 X Hyperspeed</pop>.  
* Want to browse lighter wireless [FPS-focused picks in general?](/hubs/mouse?genre=FPS&connection=wireless&weight=49-80)
pros: Big, palm-friendly ergonomic shell that especially suits larger hands
10 programmable buttons + onboard profiles make ability-heavy games and macros easy
Tri-mode flexibility: Slipstream 2.4GHz, Bluetooth, or wired play-while-charging
Responsive, modern-enough sensor performance for everyday gaming
Strong software feature set for remaps/macros and lighting (best on 2.4GHz or wired)
cons: Heavy feel can be a dealbreaker for low-DPI flick-heavy FPS play
Battery life can feel average if you stay on 2.4GHz with RGB enabled
Recurring user feedback mentions scroll-wheel quirks over time (worth factoring into the buy)
